nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; sheltered coastal reefs with steep coral-rich drop-offs. FishBase records a depth range of 0–45 m, typically 3–25 m. Generally observed singly or in small groups grazing surface algal films and microfauna from hard substrate.

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to graze surface algal films, detritus, and small microfauna from live rock. Marine-based seaweed (nori) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been reported to accept algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items (mysis) alongside tankmates; vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly recommended to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

summary: A small Western Pacific bristletooth surgeonfish with a dark brown body, golden-yellow dorsal and anal fin tips, and a white caudal fin. Generally considered one of the more peaceable and beginner-friendly tangs, well suited to medium-sized reef systems.
careNotes:
The Bristletooth Tomini Tang (Ctenochaetus tominiensis) is one of the smaller members of the bristletooth surgeonfish genus, native to sheltered reefs of the Western Pacific (Indonesia, Philippines, Papua New Guinea, northern Great Barrier Reef, Solomon Islands, Palau, Vanuatu, Fiji, Tonga). FishBase records a maximum size of 16.0 cm SL (~17 cm TL); retail sources commonly state about 6 inches (~15 cm). The species is sometimes traded as 'Flame Fin Tomini Tang' in reference to its golden-orange fin tips.

A tank of around 300 L (~80 gal) with a long horizontal footprint is generally advised. Retail sources span 70 gal (265 L, LiveAquaria/Harry's Marine Life) to 75 gal (284 L, Aquatics Unlimited). The cautious-larger choice of 300 L reflects that bristletooth tangs are active grazers throughout the day and benefit from open swimming space combined with extensive live rock for natural grazing routes. The Tomini is widely regarded as one of the most tractable tangs for medium-sized reefs and is commonly recommended as a 'starter tang.'

Water parameters should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef ranges (SG ~1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12). Temperature is commonly recommended around 24–27°C; LiveAquaria specifies 72–78°F (22–26°C) and FishBase's modelled preferred range is ~26.7–28.9°C with a mean near 27.9°C — averaged to a practical 24–27°C aquarium target.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week are generally advised; vitamin-rich diets are commonly suggested to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition this species (like most Acanthurids) is reported to develop in poor water quality or on a vitamin-deficient diet.

The species is generally considered reef-safe, and is best kept singly per tank — conspecific aggression toward other Ctenochaetus and similarly shaped tangs has been reported, but aggression toward unrelated reef fish is typically low compared with other tangs. Juveniles display tan body with yellow, blue, and white highlights; adults develop a more solid dark-brown body color with golden-yellow dorsal and anal fin tips, golden flecks above the eyes, and a white-to-pale-blue tail.

As an acanthurid, this species possesses sharp caudal-peduncle blades (the 'scalpel') used for defence. They are not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts; handle with care during transfers.

Cross-source notes: Adult max size ranged from ~15 cm (retail '~6 in') to ~17 cm TL (FishBase 16 cm SL). Tank minimum spread 70–75 gal across retail sources; per the cautious-larger rule rounded to 300 L reflecting the species' active grazing behaviour. Temperature spread 22–29°C across sources; averaged to 24–27°C. Care level was split (LiveAquaria/Aquatics Unlimited 'moderate', Harry's Marine Life 'easy to moderate') — recorded as intermediate per the ordinal-average rule.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, Ctenochaetus tominiensis is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild, with planktonic larvae; spawning in home aquaria is considered highly unlikely given tank-size and pair-bonding constraints.

Confirmed max size (6 in / 15 cm), minimum tank (70 gal / 265 L), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (easy to moderate), semi-aggressive, reef compatible.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, presumed pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild; spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely.
